title: SElinux denial with pasta when using non-default network
body:
### Issue Description

Starting some time ago (atleast some weeks ago, I didn't notice immediately) some containers failed to start. On the surface it looked like a permission error:

```
Jun 19 19:05:04 nuc.welter.lan podman[1540]: 2025-06-19 19:05:04.421050946 +0200 CEST m=+0.511412041 system refresh
Jun 19 19:05:04 nuc.welter.lan podman[1540]: 2025-06-19 19:05:04.528224539 +0200 CEST m=+0.618585634 volume create f40557fdf67662f46a6b353817d30394ada524d0b98851ec00d405cffde2829b
Jun 19 19:05:04 nuc.welter.lan podman[1540]: 2025-06-19 19:05:04.44877864 +0200 CEST m=+0.539139735 image pull 70dc580695b9bd2908c49f67e3100ccefd160a07164bdf475e76177e85383444 docker.io/jacobalberty/unifi:v8
Jun 19 19:05:04 nuc.welter.lan podman[1540]: 2025-06-19 19:05:04.564250981 +0200 CEST m=+0.654612076 container create b151472fb75ada95304ef7c1af0c48335bea1905a35a8c8ce1eccd93436d171f (image=docker.io/jacobalberty/unifi:v8, name=unifi, org.opencontainers.image.revision=74c76a44020889cb2dbdcbe9a8485afe46e820f2, org.opencontainers.image.source=https://github.com/jacobalberty/unifi-docker, maintainer=Jacob Alberty <jacob.alberty@foundigital.com>, org.opencontainers.image.licenses=MIT, org.opencontainers.image.description=Unifi Docker files, org.opencontainers.image.created=2024-11-10T12:42:27.162Z, PODMAN_SYSTEMD_UNIT=unifi.service, org.opencontainers.image.url=https://github.com/jacobalberty/unifi-docker, org.opencontainers.image.ref.name=ubuntu, io.containers.autoupdate=registry, org.opencontainers.image.title=unifi-docker, org.opencontainers.image.version=v8.6.9)
Jun 19 19:05:04 nuc.welter.lan podman[1540]: 2025-06-19 19:05:04.772399502 +0200 CEST m=+0.862760597 container remove b151472fb75ada95304ef7c1af0c48335bea1905a35a8c8ce1eccd93436d171f (image=docker.io/jacobalberty/unifi:v8, name=unifi, PODMAN_SYSTEMD_UNIT=unifi.service, io.containers.autoupdate=registry, org.opencontainers.image.title=unifi-docker, org.opencontainers.image.version=v8.6.9, org.opencontainers.image.created=2024-11-10T12:42:27.162Z, org.opencontainers.image.revision=74c76a44020889cb2dbdcbe9a8485afe46e820f2, org.opencontainers.image.source=https://github.com/jacobalberty/unifi-docker, org.opencontainers.image.url=https://github.com/jacobalberty/unifi-docker, maintainer=Jacob Alberty <jacob.alberty@foundigital.com>, org.opencontainers.image.description=Unifi Docker files, org.opencontainers.image.ref.name=ubuntu, org.opencontainers.image.licenses=MIT)
Jun 19 19:05:04 nuc.welter.lan podman[1540]: 2025-06-19 19:05:04.804979726 +0200 CEST m=+0.895340820 volume remove f40557fdf67662f46a6b353817d30394ada524d0b98851ec00d405cffde2829b
Jun 19 19:05:04 nuc.welter.lan podman[1540]: Error: setting up Pasta: pasta failed with exit code 1:
Jun 19 19:05:04 nuc.welter.lan podman[1540]: Couldn't open PID file /tmp/containers-user-1003/containers/networks/rootless-netns/rootless-netns-conn.pid: Permission denied
Jun 19 19:05:04 nuc.welter.lan systemd[1093]: unifi.service: Main process exited, code=exited, status=126/n/a
```

After digging deeper I found that it was actually SElinux denying pasta to create/read the PID file:

```
type=AVC msg=audit(1750358869.815:377): avc:  denied  { search } for  pid=5416 comm="pasta" name="containers-user-1003" dev="tmpfs" ino=33 scontext=unconfined_u:unconfined_r:pasta_t:s0-s0:c0.c1023 tcontext=unconfined_u:object_r:container_var_run_t:s0 tclass=dir permissive=0
```

Temporarily setting SElinux to permissive made the containers start again. So setting SElinux to enforcing again and creating and loading the following SElinux policy made the containers start all the time:

```
module mypasta 1.0;

require {
	type container_var_run_t;
	type pasta_t;
	class dir { add_name open read search watch write };
	class file { create open write };
}

#============= pasta_t ==============

allow pasta_t container_var_run_t:dir { add_name open read search watch write };
allow pasta_t container_var_run_t:file { create open write };
```

But needing this policy all of the sudden kept bugging me. Searching bugzillas, this project, etc  I found no similar recent problems so it must be a local issue. Then I finally noticed the difference between the failing containers and non-failing container: The failing containers were not using the default network. For more isolation I had created a podman network per container. This had always worked until one of the weekly updates in the past few weeks (months?) made the containers fail to start but I didn't notice because they run on a headless server and automatically start on boot. Editing the systemd unit file to not use the '--network' parameter made the failing containers start again.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. Install Fedora Silverblue
2. Create custom podman network (podman network create <name>)
3. Start container with `--network <name>` parameter
4. Running an network isolated container


### Describe the results you received

Container fails to start due to permission/SElinux errors.

### Describe the results you expected

Running containers using isolated networks. The podman config hasn't been changed but started failed after applying host (Fedora Silverblue 42) updates somewhere in the past weeks.

### podman info output

```yaml

  arch: amd64
  buildahVersion: 1.40.1

  - cpuset

  conmon:
    package: conmon-2.1.13-1.fc42.x86_64

    version: 'conmon version 2.1.13, commit: '
  cpuUtilization:
    idlePercent: 98.59
    systemPercent: 0.57
    userPercent: 0.84
  cpus: 4

  distribution:
    distribution: fedora

  idMappings:
    gidmap: null
    uidmap: null
  kernel: 6.14.11-300.fc42.x86_64
  linkmode: dynamic
  logDriver: journald
  memFree: 13471715328
  memTotal: 16160579584
  networkBackend: netavark
  networkBackendInfo:
    backend: netavark
    dns:
      package: aardvark-dns-1.15.0-1.fc42.x86_64
      path: /usr/libexec/podman/aardvark-dns
      version: aardvark-dns 1.15.0
    package: netavark-1.15.2-1.fc42.x86_64
    path: /usr/libexec/podman/netavark
    version: netavark 1.15.2
  ociRuntime:
    name: crun
    package: crun-1.21-1.fc42.x86_64
    path: /usr/bin/crun
    version: |-
      crun version 1.21
      commit: 10269840aa07fb7e6b7e1acff6198692d8ff5c88
      rundir: /run/crun
      spec: 1.0.0
      +SYSTEMD +SELINUX +APPARMOR +CAP +SECCOMP +EBPF +CRIU +LIBKRUN +WASM:wasmedge +YAJL
  os: linux
  pasta:
    executable: /usr/sbin/pasta
    package: passt-0^20250611.g0293c6f-1.fc42.x86_64
    version: |
      pasta 0^20250611.g0293c6f-1.fc42.x86_64-pasta
      Copyright Red Hat
      GNU General Public License, version 2 or later
        <https://www.gnu.org/licenses/old-licenses/gpl-2.0.html>
      This is free software: you are free to change and redistribute it.
      There is NO WARRANTY, to the extent permitted by law.
  remoteSocket:
    exists: true

  rootlessNetworkCmd: pasta
  security:
    apparmorEnabled: false
    capabilities: CAP_CHOWN,CAP_DAC_OVERRIDE,CAP_FOWNER,CAP_FSETID,CAP_KILL,CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE,CAP_SETFCAP,CAP_SETGID,CAP_SETPCAP,CAP_SETUID,CAP_SYS_CHROOT
    rootless: false
    seccompEnabled: true
    seccompProfilePath: /usr/share/containers/seccomp.json
    selinuxEnabled: true
  serviceIsRemote: false
  slirp4netns:
    executable: /usr/sbin/slirp4netns

      libseccomp: 2.5.5
  swapFree: 8589930496

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

None

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

Running baremetal on a Fedora Silverblue host.

### Additional information

Additional information like issue happens only occasionally or issue happens with a particular architecture or on a particular setting

comments.body: @sbrivio-rh PTAL

Thanks @GerbenWelter for reporting this! This is the same as  https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=2373054. As it only happens for containers that were started before a recent upgrade of passt-selinux, a workaround would be to restart them.

I would suggest to track this on the existing Fedora ticket, but we can also leave this one open if it's practical, and I'll take care of closing it once the issue is fixed.

@sbrivio-rh  Ahh, I failed to find that one, but because I use Fedora Silverblue I need to reboot to use the updated packages. So the containers have definitely been restarted before. Naturally I also restarted the containers during troubleshooting. So rebooting/restarting doesn't fix this issue.

Oops, sorry, according to https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=2373054#c3 the only way to fix this for existing containers is  `podman system reset`. Well, you can also do, as root (untested, but I'm fairly sure it should work, minus typos):

    chcon system_u:object_r:pasta_pid_t:s0 /tmp/storage-run-1003/containers/networks/rootless-netns/rootless-netns-conn.pid

The path you mentioned (and was also mentioned in the bugzilla) was not present on my system but a similar one was so I adapted your command to:
```
chcon system_u:object_r:pasta_pid_t:s0 /tmp/containers-user-1003/containers/networks/rootless-netns
```
(no pid file, directory was empty)

but that did not fix the problem. So I did `podman system reset`, recreated the extra podman network and the container started again. I did notice after the container was running again the directory `/tmp/containers-user-1003/containers/networks` was not present anymore. So I guess there was a mismatch between podman and the container definition and that's why the reset was needed?

So the fix is clear. If needed I can leave another container in the same state so I can gather additional information if needed. Just let me know.

> /tmp/containers-user-1003/containers

That is general a sign that the first time you used podman on that user you had no proper systemd login session (no $XDG_RUNTIME_DIR defined). Since the root/runnroot should never changed podman then caches that "incorrect" dir forever (well until podman system reset). Now with the proper XDG_RUNTIME_DIR runroot it starts working as that seems to have the proper labels.

What I don't get here or in https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=2373054 why is the rundir labelled container_var_run_t? I don't see a rule describing that and by default /tmp paths seems to have user_tmp_t which is what we would expect and where the transition rule would relabel the paths properly

I'm afraid I got hit by this issue as well (at least I think as a see a few entries in SELinux logs).
I'm wondering if I should rollback or run `podman system reset`.

1. How destructive is this command? I only use bind mounts so containers data should be safe but I rather check here before I proceed.
2. Do I have to stop all containers before performing the reset?
3. I use only run rootless containers, do I need to run this command as root or not?

A simple reboot should be enough, if the selinux labels are still incorrect after a reboot something really strange is happening and we need to figure out why some users have different labels on the /tmp paths.
In any case what is the label on the runroot dir `ls -Zd $(podman info --format {{.Store.RunRoot}})`? Before and after the reboot, is there any difference?

`podman system reset` must be run as the same user and will delete all containers, images, volumes, ... So if you have important volumes (`podman volume ls`) then make sure they are backed up. However if you use bind mounts then these directories are not deleted.

I don't know if this is safe but it seems to work for me instead of using ```podman system reset``` which was too destructive for my situation. I did the following and it seemed to correct the RunRoot location for me.

1. Go to https://github.com/br0xen/boltbrowser/releases
2. Download the latest Linux binary (e.g. boltbrowser.linux64)
3. Make it executable:  ```chmod +x boltbrowser.linux64```
4. Shutdown all containers (in my case, when I shut them down they are removed)
5. Backup current ```~/.local/share/containers/storage/libpod/bolt_state.db``` file (just in case)
6. Edit bolt_state.db

```./boltbrowser.linux64 ~/.local/share/containers/storage/libpod/bolt_state.db```

7. Navigate to ```+ runtime-config``` and press Enter to expand it.
8. Navigate to ```run-root``` and press Enter to edit its value.
9. Change from /tmp path to /run/user path.
10. Navigate to ```tmp-dir``` and press Enter to edit its value.
11. Change from /tmp/run-[uid]/libpod/tmp to /run/user/[uid]/libpod/tmp
12. Exit boltbrowser

In my case once I finished this and then started my containers, they didn't have the permissions errror from SELinux and their files were no longer being put under /tmp and instead were under /run/user.

Hope that is helpful to someone else who doesn't want to to ```podman system reset```.

> I don't know if this is safe but it seems to work for me instead of using `podman system reset` which was too destructive for my situation. I did the following and it seemed to correct the RunRoot location for me.
> 
>  1. Go to https://github.com/br0xen/boltbrowser/releases
> 
>  2. Download the latest Linux binary (e.g. boltbrowser.linux64)
> 
>  3. Make it executable:  `chmod +x boltbrowser.linux64`
> 
>  4. Shutdown all containers (in my case, when I shut them down they are removed)
> 
>  5. Backup current `~/.local/share/containers/storage/libpod/bolt_state.db` file (just in case)
> 
>  6. Edit bolt_state.db
> 
>     `./boltbrowser.linux64 ~/.local/share/containers/storage/libpod/bolt_state.db`
> 
>  7. Navigate to `+ runtime-config` and press Enter to expand it.
> 
>  8. Navigate to `run-root` and press Enter to edit its value.
> 
>  9. Change from /tmp path to /run/user path.
> 
>  10. Navigate to `tmp-dir` and press Enter to edit its value.
> 
>  11. Change from /tmp/run-[uid]/libpod/tmp to /run/user/[uid]/libpod/tmp
> 
>  12. Exit boltbrowser
> 
> In my case once I finished this and then started my containers, they didn't have the permissions errror from SELinux and their files were no longer being put under /tmp and instead were under /run/user.
> 
> Hope that is helpful to someone else who doesn't want to to `podman system reset`.

Hmm, I wonder if it would be useful to add a `--run-root` flag to `podman system migrate` that would handle this semi-automatically for you?

The issue is not the rundir per see, the selinux transition rules take care of the right labels regardless.

The issue here is that your runroot is labelled incorrectly with container_var_run_t instead of the regular expected user_tmp_t, so until someone can explain to me why your temporary dir is labelled like that I am not sure what we can do.

> I did the following and it seemed to correct the RunRoot location for me.
> 
>     1. Go to https://github.com/br0xen/boltbrowser/releases
> 
> [...]

> Hmm, I wonder if it would be useful to add a `--run-root` flag to `podman system migrate` that would handle this semi-automatically for you?

...as Paul mentioned, we need to figure out why this issue occurs and how to fix it. I appreciate the workarounds but, given that I'm unable to reproduce the problem, I'm currently not sure what the fix might be, so if somebody currently hitting the problem could find out where that directory label comes from, this would be even more appreciated. That is, the question from https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=2373054#c15 is still open.

If somebody can answer that, you win a fix and a same-day package update.

For us this problem showed up right after updating packages on Fedora CoreOS, although we are using the default network:

>   passt 0^20250320.g32f6212-2.fc42 -> 0^20250606.g754c6d7-1.fc42
  passt-selinux 0^20250320.g32f6212-2.fc42 -> 0^20250606.g754c6d7-1.fc42
  podman 5:5.4.1-1.fc42 -> 5:5.5.1-1.fc42

After rebooting, our application which is running in a podman container and started through systemd did start, but our application was no longer able to create/start any containers (with the default network) via the podman socket as it resulted in the same error:
> pasta failed with exit code 1:
Couldn't open PID file /tmp/storage-run-1002/containers/networks/rootless-netns/rootless-netns-conn.pid: Permission denied

With the following accompanying audit log error:
> type=AVC msg=audit(1751285978.770:328): avc:  denied  { search } for  pid=6250 comm="pasta.avx2" name="storage-run-1002" dev="tmpfs" ino=23 scontext=unconfined_u:unconfined_r:pasta_t:s0-s0:c0.c1023 tcontext=unconfined_u:object_r:container_var_run_t:s0 tclass=dir permissive=0

> [user@pc ~]$ ls -Zd $(podman info --format {{.Store.RunRoot}})
unconfined_u:object_r:container_var_run_t:s0 /tmp/storage-run-1002/containers

A `podman system reset` did resolve the issue, after which the runRoot also changed from `/tmp/storage-run-1002/containers` to `/run/user/1002/containers`. 

I hope to have some time by the end of the week to try and reproduce this once again, as we have multiple machines running the same software. Does anyone have any points on things I should look at before and after trying to update packages on another system?

@Goz3rr

> After rebooting, our application which is running in a podman container and started through systemd did start, but our application was no longer able to create/start any containers (with the default network) via the podman socket as it resulted in the same error:
> 
> > pasta failed with exit code 1:
> > Couldn't open PID file /tmp/storage-run-1002/containers/networks/rootless-netns/rootless-netns-conn.pid: Permission denied
> 
> With the following accompanying audit log error:
> 
> > type=AVC msg=audit(1751285978.770:328): avc:  denied  { search } for  pid=6250 comm="pasta.avx2" name="storage-run-1002" dev="tmpfs" ino=23 scontext=unconfined_u:unconfined_r:pasta_t:s0-s0:c0.c1023 tcontext=unconfined_u:object_r:container_var_run_t:s0 tclass=dir permissive=0

Ah, I was hoping/assuming that a reboot would fix the problem, but it appears not. 

> Does anyone have any points on things I should look at before and after trying to update packages on another system?

Does running 

```console
# semanage fcontext --add '/tmp/storage-run-[^/]+/containers/networks/rootless-netns' --type ifconfig_var_run_t
```

solve the problem? If that doesn't solve the problem, then does running

```console
# restorecon -vR /tmp/storage-run-*/containers/networks/
```

fix it? I wouldn't expect for the first command to fix things, and the second command will probably only solve the problem until the next reboot, but there's a chance that this is the solution.

Also, what is the output of running

```console
$ ls -laZ /tmp/storage-run-1002/containers/networks/
```

Ok I found a reproducer

```
tmpdir=$(mktemp -d)
systemd-run --user -q --wait --pipe podman --root $tmpdir/root --runroot $tmpdir/containers --tmpdir $tmpdir/tmp  run --rm --network bridge  quay.io/libpod/testimage:20241011 true
```

The key point is that this only happens when run as systemd service AND `containers` is part of the runroot name which it is by default when not specifying --runroot.

I am not sure on the selinux details here because podman definitely runs as `unconfined_u:unconfined_r:container_runtime_t:s0-s0:c0.c1023` but systemd I think starts it as unconfined_service_t and that triggers a special transition rule
However if you look at container-selinux is has this rule
```
files_pid_filetrans($1, container_var_run_t, dir, "containers")
```
https://github.com/containers/container-selinux/blob/36e8f213b7ac8a1843e5e37b37eb8ef7bdc2af9c/container.if#L512 added in https://github.com/containers/container-selinux/commit/e06212c96d71174bf4085f8e3704b4805c668625

And then calls the interface with `container_filetrans_named_content(unconfined_service_t)`

Not sure what the purpose of this is but I would argue relabelling our own runroot to container_var_run_t seems wrong
cc @rhatdan @lsm5

@Luap99 

> Ok I found a reproducer
> 
> ```
> tmpdir=$(mktemp -d)
> systemd-run --user -q --wait --pipe podman --root $tmpdir/root --runroot $tmpdir/containers --tmpdir $tmpdir/tmp  run --rm --network bridge  quay.io/libpod/testimage:20241011 true
> ```

Hmm, that command works correctly on my system—Podman/`systemd-run` exits with no errors, and there are no SELinux denials in my logs.

```console
$ rpm --query --queryformat='%{NAME}\t%{VERSION}\n' containers-common container-selinux libselinux passt passt-selinux podman policycoreutils selinux-policy selinux-policy-targeted | column --table
containers-common        0.63.1
container-selinux        2.238.0
libselinux               3.8
libselinux               3.8
passt                    0^20250611.gc40ab6c
passt-selinux            0^20250611.gc40ab6c
podman                   5.5.2
policycoreutils          3.8
selinux-policy           41.44
selinux-policy-targeted  41.44
```

> The key point is that this only happens when run as systemd service AND `containers` is part of the runroot name which it is by default when not specifying --runroot.
> 
> I am not sure on the selinux details here because podman definitely runs as `unconfined_u:unconfined_r:container_runtime_t:s0-s0:c0.c1023` but systemd I think starts it as unconfined_service_t and that triggers a special transition rule However if you look at container-selinux is has this rule
> 
> ```
> files_pid_filetrans($1, container_var_run_t, dir, "containers")
> ```
> 
> https://github.com/containers/container-selinux/blob/36e8f213b7ac8a1843e5e37b37eb8ef7bdc2af9c/container.if#L512 added in [containers/container-selinux@e06212c](https://github.com/containers/container-selinux/commit/e06212c96d71174bf4085f8e3704b4805c668625)
> 
> And then calls the interface with `container_filetrans_named_content(unconfined_service_t)`

The pasta SELinux policy only handles the case where the runroot is labelled as `user_tmp_t`:

```sepolicy
type_transition container_runtime_t user_tmp_t : dir ifconfig_var_run_t "rootless-netns";
```

But in this case, it sounds like the runroot is actually labelled as `container_var_run_t`. Does adding 

```sepolicy
type_transition container_runtime_t container_var_run_t : dir ifconfig_var_run_t "rootless-netns";
```

solve the problem? If so, that's a fairly easy fix for pasta, but the runroot should probably have the same label regardless of whether it's at `/tmp/storage-run-%{USERID}/containers` or `/run/user/%{USERID}/containers`.

> Hmm, that command works correctly on my system—Podman/systemd-run exits with no errors, and there are no SELinux denials in my logs.

So the actual reproducer:
```
tmpdir=$(mktemp -d)
rm -rf $tmpdir 
systemd-run --user -q --wait --pipe podman --root $tmpdir/root --runroot $tmpdir/containers --tmpdir $tmpdir/tmp  run --rm --network bridge  quay.io/libpod/testimage:20241011 true
```

Not sure why it matter if the tmpdir already exists. The podman command will have to mkdir root/runroot directories inside anyway...

> The pasta SELinux policy only handles the case where the runroot is labelled as user_tmp_t:

Yes and I think that is fine. I don't know why we have all these rules in containers-selinux but I would assume the fact that is labels the directory as  `container_var_run_t` is the real bug and we should not work around that in the pasta policy.

> Ah, I was hoping/assuming that a reboot would fix the problem, but it appears not.

Just a small update, after a clean reinstall on one of our devices with our Fedora CoreOS based image a coworker ran into the same issue, so at least I have an image I can use to mess around with for a repro. On this machine a reboot did solve the issue, while it did not on the machine I initially had the problem on.

I did some more digging, all I see is that container_var_run_t says this:

> - Set files with the container_var_run_t type, if you want to store the container files under the /run or /var/run directory.

And it does seem to have a rule for the default rootful runroot
`/run/containers(/.*)?		gen_context(system_u:object_r:container_var_run_t,s0)`

But not for the rootless one `/run/user/$UID/containers` which is always user_tmp_t.

What I do notice though the issue only happen when podman creates the tmpdir, i.e. it didn't already exist:
```
$ runcon --type container_runtime_t mkdir $XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/test1
$ ls -Zd $XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/test1
unconfined_u:object_r:user_tmp_t:s0 /run/user/1000/test1
```

but
```
$ runcon --type container_runtime_t mkdir /tmp/test1
$ ls -Zd /tmp/test1/
unconfined_u:object_r:container_var_run_t:s0 /tmp/test1/

$ mkdir /tmp/test
$ ls -Zd /tmp/test/
unconfined_u:object_r:user_tmp_t:s0 /tmp/test/
$ runcon --type container_runtime_t mkdir /tmp/test/test1
$ ls -Zd /tmp/test/test1/
unconfined_u:object_r:user_tmp_t:s0 /tmp/test/test1/
```
So basically the issue only ever happens when podman creates the first level directory under /tmp, it doesn't seem to be related to systemd as I thought before. So what rule defines that the first dir under /tmp is labelled with container_var_run_t. 
And because the first directory label is wrong selinux then keeps using the wrong parent context for all files/directories created under it unless there are further transition rules.

@rhatdan @lsm5 Can you please advise here, what is the expected label for the runroot (container_var_run_t or user_tmp_t)? Do we have to allow both in the pasta policy? I would assume the label podman uses should be consistent.

> I have not studied this but the issue is the "containers" directory under /tmp is being created with the wrong label, correct?

@rhatdan I think the main question is what is the supposed label of the podman runroot supposed to be fist. I cannot say what correct is, I can only say it is different from the label used for $XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/containers.
So should be container_var_run_t or user_tmp_t? Because the behavior differs for root vs rootless and if you use a path under /tmp.

```
$ sudo ls -dZ /run/containers/
unconfined_u:object_r:container_var_run_t:s0 /run/containers/
$ ls -dZ /run/user/1000/containers/
unconfined_u:object_r:user_tmp_t:s0 /run/user/1000/containers/
```

Whatever the answer to that question is means we need to update container-selinux to label the runroot consistently with it and then we can have the pasta policy update to use the right base label for the transition.

So looking at the policy I think the reason is we have
```
type_transition container_runtime_t tmp_t:dir container_var_run_t;
```
but no rule for user_tmp_t which is the label of the $XDG_RUNTIME_DIR

Also the policy seems to define a container_runtime_tmp_t and container_runtime_tmpfs_t types as well I am not sure what they should be used for then? The man page description doesn't really help me.

container_runtime_tmp_t
> Set files with the container_runtime_tmp_t type, if you want to store container runtime temporary files in the /tmp directories.

container_runtime_tmpfs_t
> Set files with the container_runtime_tmpfs_t type, if you want to store container runtime files on a tmpfs file system.

container_var_run_t
> Set files with the container_var_run_t type, if you want to store the container files under the /run or /var/run directory.

And it also defines
```
/run/containers(/.*)?		gen_context(system_u:object_r:container_var_run_t,s0)
```
But no rule for /run/user/UID/containers
It looks like it should be user_tmp_t. At least this is what I see under /run/user/UID ...
This was fixed in the pasta policy here https://archives.passt.top/passt-dev/20250917120450.36181-2-pholzing@redhat.com/ which went into 2025_09_19.623dbf6

That doesn't seem to have solved.
```
$ sudo ls -dZ /run/user/1000/containers
unconfined_u:object_r:user_tmp_t:s0 /run/user/1000/containers
$ sudo restorecon -v /run/user/1000/containers
Warning no default label for /run/user/1000/containers
$ rpm -q passt
passt-0^20250919.g623dbf6-1.fc42.x86_64
```

> That doesn't seem to have solved.
> 
> ```
> $ sudo ls -dZ /run/user/1000/containers
> unconfined_u:object_r:user_tmp_t:s0 /run/user/1000/containers
> $ sudo restorecon -v /run/user/1000/containers
> Warning no default label for /run/user/1000/containers
> $ rpm -q passt
> passt-0^20250919.g623dbf6-1.fc42.x86_64
> ```

Because we did not change this, I fixed the pasta policy to accept both user_tmp_t and container_var_run_t so this bug here is fixed. Whenever the container run dir should be container_var_run_t is up to debate for contianer-selinux and I have yet to receive a proper explanation what each label in the container policy are supposed to do.

In a perfect world content under /run would be labeled ***_var_run_t. Except for user content stored under /run/user which is labeled user_tmp_t.

```
ls -lZ /run/user
total 0
drwx------. 19 dwalsh dwalsh unconfined_u:object_r:user_tmp_t:s0 620 Oct 20 09:30 3267
ramalama (artifact) $ matchpathcon /run/user
/run/user	system_u:object_r:user_tmp_t:s0
```

Allowing pasta to create content under user_tmp_t is the correct way to handle this.

> Allowing pasta to create content under user_tmp_t is the correct way to handle this.

That's already allowed, and has been from the beginning.

I don't think there's an issue left after Paul's series with changes to pasta's policy, other than the fact that we don't yet have an answer to this question, quoting from https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/26473#issuecomment-2995703074:

> What I don't get here or in https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=2373054 why is the rundir labelled container_var_run_t? I don't see a rule describing that and by default /tmp paths seems to have user_tmp_t which is what we would expect and where the transition rule would relabel the paths properly
